title: About Me
text:
I grew up in Siliguri, West Bengal, India, a picturesque city in the foothills of the Himalayas, where I completed my schooling before moving to Kolkata, West Bengal, India, to pursue a Bachelor’s degree in Physics at Jadavpur University. I then joined the Harish-Chandra Research Institute (HRI), Prayagraj, India, for an Integrated M.Sc.-Ph.D., where I began my research in quantum information theory.
My academic journey subsequently took me to Europe, first as a postdoctoral researcher at Jagiellonian University in Kraków, Poland, and later at the Abdus Salam International Centre for Theoretical Physics (ICTP) in Trieste, Italy. During this period, my research interests gradually expanded from quantum information theory to quantum many-body physics and condensed matter theory. In 2023, after nearly five years abroad, I returned to India to join the Indian Institute of Technology Indore as an Assistant Professor, before moving to the Indian Institute of Technology Madras in 2024, where I currently lead the Synthetic Quantum Systems (SQS) research group at the Department of Physics.

title: Numerical Libraries
text:
I actively develop open-source scientific software for quantum many-body physics and quantum information. The libraries below are freely available and are used in my research.

QIClib : A C++ library for general purpose quantum computing and quantum information.
TenNetLib.jl : A Julia library for tensor network algorithms, designed for large-scale simulations of strongly correlated quantum many-body systems.







QIClib







TenNetlib.jl

title: Research
text:
Our research aims to understand how interactions between many quantum particles give rise to emergent, collective phenomena that have no counterpart in individual constituents. We investigate strongly correlated quantum systems with interests ranging from quantum criticality and topological phases to lattice gauge theories, nonequilibrium dynamics, and many-body localization.
Addressing these problems requires both analytical insight and powerful computational techniques. We develop and employ tensor network algorithms alongside quantum field theory and different many-body techniques to study systems beyond the reach of conventional numerical approaches, while also drawing on concepts from quantum information theory to characterize complex quantum states.
A central theme of our research is quantum simulation using synthetic quantum systems. We explore how ultracold atoms, Rydberg atom arrays, trapped ions, and cavity QED platforms can realize exotic many-body Hamiltonians and provide experimentally accessible settings for studying fundamental problems in condensed matter physics and beyond.
For a comprehensive list of publications, please visit my Google Scholar or browse preprints and accepted manuscripts on arXiv.

title: Synthetic Quantum Systems (SQS) @ IIT Madras
text:
The Group of Synthetic Quantum Systems (SQS) is always looking for motivated members for working in the inter-disciplinary areas involving Quantum Many-Body Physics, Quantum Simulations, Condensed Matter Physics, and Quantum Information Science.
How many quantum physicists does it take to split a restaurant bill?
If you want to know the answer to the above question and are interested in joining the group to pursue your Ph.D., please get in touch by sending an email to titas.chanda [at] physics.iitm.ac.in, attaching your updated CV.
Ph.D. Candidates with CSIR/UGC NET JRF will be preferred.
We also encourage interested Postdoctoral Candidates to apply for a ANRF-National Post-Doctoral Fellowship with us. If you are interested, please get in touch via email to titas.chanda [at] physics.iitm.ac.in, attaching your updated CV.
ITM students interested in working with me on a project are encouraged to email their CV to my physics.iitm.ac.in address. Since I usually travel during summer and winter vacations, I do not offer project internships to students outside IITM.

title: Titas Chanda
text:
Assistant Professor
Department of Physics
IIT Madras
 Chennai, India

 Google Scholar
 ORCID
 arXiv
 GitHub
 Linkedin







I am Titas Chanda, an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics at the Indian Institute of Technology Madras (IIT Madras), where I lead the Synthetic Quantum Systems (SQS) research group. I am also affiliated with the Center for Quantum Information Communication and Computing (CQuICC), IIT Madras.
My research broadly spans condensed matter theory, quantum many-body physics, quantum information science, and quantum simulation. I am particularly interested in uncovering the fundamental principles that govern complex quantum systems through a combination of analytical and numerical approaches.
My current interests include strongly correlated quantum systems, tensor network methods, critical phenomena, lattice gauge theories, topological phases of matter, out-of-equilibrium dynamics, and more. These topics are investigated in a variety of physical settings, including ultracold atoms in optical lattices, Rydberg atom arrays, trapped ions, cavity QED setups, and other emerging synthetic quantum systems.
More details about my research can be found on the Research page.

title: Titas Chanda
text:
Recent News

June 2026
Welcome to my new website! This site replaces my previous Google Sites page. The new platform offers greater flexibility and will be regularly updated with research updates, publications, teaching materials, and other relevant news.


April 2026

Sourabh and Neelanjana Dey, our PhD students in the SQS group, attended the ICTP-IBM School on Quantum Computing and Simulation at ICTP, Trieste, Italy from April 20 to 30, 2026. The two-week school offered lecture courses by leading researchers on quantum computing, quantum simulation, and related topics.





January 2026

2025 was an exciting year for our research, marked by publications in several leading journals, including Reports on Progress in Physics, Physical Review X, and Physical Review Letters.
- A review article in Reports on Progress in Physics surveyed recent theoretical and experimental advances in non-standard Bose–Hubbard models, with a particular emphasis on long-range interactions and quantum simulation with ultracold atoms.
- A Physical Review X paper developed a theoretical framework for fractional quantum Hall states coupled to quantum light, revealing cavity-induced topological phenomena, including graviton-polaritons and light-matter entanglement.
- A Physical Review Letters paper demonstrated that chiral graviton modes survive in lattice fractional quantum Hall systems, providing a realistic route for their observation in quantum simulation experiments.
- Another Physical Review Letters paper uncovered emergent phases and unconventional critical phenomena in frustrated clock models, revealing a novel mechanism for the emergence of forbidden discrete symmetries at long wavelengths.
